news_summary: Advancements in virtual biological modeling, innovative materials for blue LEDs, targeted enzyme discovery, and a landmark global assessment of AI highlight significant progress in technology and science.
news_summary_fr: Les avancées en matière de modélisation biologique virtuelle, les matériaux innovants pour les LED bleues, la découverte ciblée d'enzymes et une évaluation mondiale historique de l'intelligence artificielle mettent en évidence des progrès significatifs dans les domaines de la technologie et de la science.
news_summary_es: Los avances en la modelización biológica virtual, los materiales innovadores para los LED azules, el descubrimiento de enzimas específicas y una evaluación mundial de referencia sobre la inteligencia artificial ponen de relieve los importantes avances en tecnología y ciencia.

articles.title: Towards the construction of a virtual yeast
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 01 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10574-9A virtual yeast, an artificial intelligence-driven agent for modelling eukaryotic cellular behaviours, is described.

Cell 150, 389–401 (2012).This pioneering study presents the first mechanistic whole-cell model that integrates all molecular processes of M. genitalium using diverse mathematical formalisms to unify fundamentally different cellular processes and experimental measurements, enabling genotype-to-phenotype prediction.

Adduri, A. K. et al. Predicting cellular responses to perturbation across diverse contexts with State. Preprint at bioRxiv https://doi.org/10.1101/2025.06.26.661135 (2025). This study introduces State, a scalable AI framework trained on perturbation transcriptomic data, from over 100 million cells, that predicts cellular responses across unseen contexts, advancing the dynamic modelling of perturbation effects central to AIVC development.

Sun, R. et al. A perturbation proteomics-based foundation model for virtual cell construction. Preprint at bioRxiv https://doi.org/10.1101/2025.02.07.637070 (2025). This study introduces ProteinTalks, a neural ordinary differential equation-based foundation model trained on 38 million perturbed protein measurements, which learns cellular protein network dynamics to predict drug efficacy, synergy and resistance, laying a proteome-centric foundation for virtual cell development.

articles.reasoning: The article describes the development of a virtual yeast model driven by artificial intelligence, representing a significant technological breakthrough in multiscale biological simulation. This advancement has broad implications for understanding cellular behavior, drug development, and biotechnology, offering measurable benefits to science and society. The article provides substantial context and references, focusing clearly on AI-driven whole-cell modeling, fulfilling criteria for inspiring good news.
articles.personality_title: Scientists create a virtual yeast cell using artificial intelligence
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists have long studied yeast cells because they are simple living organisms that help us understand how cells work. Yeast is used in many areas, like baking, brewing, and medicine. Recently, researchers have tried to build computer models that can simulate an entire yeast cell to predict how it behaves.

**What happened** – A team of scientists has developed a new virtual yeast model powered by artificial intelligence (AI). This model combines many types of biological data and advanced computer techniques to simulate the complex activities inside a yeast cell. It uses information about yeast genes, proteins, metabolism, and cell structures to create a detailed digital version of the cell that can predict how it will respond to changes.

**Impact** – This virtual yeast model is important because it can help scientists understand cell functions better without needing to do as many experiments in the lab. It can speed up drug development by predicting how yeast cells react to different substances. It also provides a new tool for biotechnology, where yeast is often used to produce medicines and other useful products. The model’s ability to simulate whole-cell behavior with AI is a big step forward in biology and computing.

**What's next step** – Researchers plan to improve this virtual yeast by adding more data and refining the AI methods. They also want to use this approach to build virtual models of other cells, including human cells. This could lead to better medicines and new ways to study diseases by testing treatments on digital cells before real ones.

**One-sentence takeaway** – Using AI, scientists have built a detailed virtual yeast cell that can simulate its inner workings, opening new paths for research and medicine.
articles.personality_title_fr: Des scientifiques créent une cellule de levure virtuelle grâce à l’intelligence artificielle
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient depuis longtemps les cellules de levure, car ce sont des organismes vivants simples qui aident à comprendre le fonctionnement des cellules. La levure est utilisée dans de nombreux domaines comme la boulangerie, la brasserie et la médecine. Récemment, les chercheurs ont essayé de construire des modèles informatiques capables de simuler une cellule de levure entière pour prédire son comportement.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Une équipe de chercheurs a développé un nouveau modèle de levure virtuelle alimenté par l’intelligence artificielle (IA). Ce modèle combine de nombreux types de données biologiques et des techniques informatiques avancées pour simuler les activités complexes à l’intérieur d’une cellule de levure. Il utilise des informations sur les gènes, les protéines, le métabolisme et la structure cellulaire pour créer une version numérique détaillée capable de prédire la réaction de la cellule aux changements.

**Impact** – Ce modèle virtuel de levure est important car il aide les scientifiques à mieux comprendre le fonctionnement des cellules sans avoir à réaliser autant d’expériences en laboratoire. Il peut accélérer le développement de médicaments en prédisant la réaction des cellules de levure à différentes substances. Il offre aussi un nouvel outil pour la biotechnologie, où la levure est souvent utilisée pour produire des médicaments et d’autres produits utiles. La capacité de simuler le comportement complet de la cellule avec l’IA est une avancée majeure en biologie et en informatique.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs prévoient d’améliorer cette levure virtuelle en ajoutant plus de données et en affinant les méthodes d’IA. Ils souhaitent aussi utiliser cette approche pour créer des modèles virtuels d’autres cellules, notamment humaines. Cela pourrait conduire à de meilleurs médicaments et à de nouvelles façons d’étudier les maladies en testant des traitements sur des cellules numériques avant les cellules réelles.

**Résumé en une phrase** – Grâce à l’IA, des scientifiques ont construit une cellule de levure virtuelle détaillée capable de simuler ses processus internes, ouvrant de nouvelles voies pour la recherche et la médecine.
articles.personality_title_es: Científicos crean una célula de levadura virtual usando inteligencia artificial
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos han estudiado las células de levadura durante mucho tiempo porque son organismos vivos simples que ayudan a entender cómo funcionan las células. La levadura se usa en la panadería, la elaboración de bebidas y la medicina. Recientemente, los investigadores han intentado construir modelos informáticos que puedan simular una célula de levadura completa para predecir su comportamiento.

**Qué pasó** – Un equipo de científicos desarrolló un nuevo modelo virtual de levadura impulsado por inteligencia artificial (IA). Este modelo combina muchos tipos de datos biológicos y técnicas informáticas avanzadas para simular las actividades complejas dentro de una célula de levadura. Utiliza información sobre genes, proteínas, metabolismo y estructuras celulares para crear una versión digital detallada que puede predecir cómo responderá la célula a los cambios.

**Impacto** – Este modelo virtual de levadura es importante porque ayuda a los científicos a comprender mejor las funciones celulares sin necesidad de hacer tantos experimentos en el laboratorio. Puede acelerar el desarrollo de medicamentos al predecir cómo reaccionan las células de levadura a diferentes sustancias. También ofrece una nueva herramienta para la biotecnología, donde la levadura se usa para producir medicinas y otros productos útiles. La capacidad del modelo para simular el comportamiento de toda la célula con IA es un gran avance en biología e informática.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores planean mejorar esta levadura virtual añadiendo más datos y perfeccionando los métodos de IA. También quieren usar este enfoque para construir modelos virtuales de otras células, incluidas las humanas. Esto podría llevar a mejores medicinas y nuevas formas de estudiar enfermedades probando tratamientos en células digitales antes que en células reales.

**Resumen en una frase** – Usando IA, científicos han creado una célula de levadura virtual detallada que simula sus procesos internos, abriendo nuevos caminos para la investigación y la medicina.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of a glowing, intricately interconnected network of yeast cells rendered as delicate, translucent spheres, each filled with stylized molecular structures and flowing data streams symbolizing AI-driven simulations and genome-scale models, set against a soft, natural background of subtle earth and cream hues.

articles.title: Isomeric multi-hydrogen-bonding enables blue perovskite LEDs
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 01 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10723-0Hydrogen-bonding networks engineered with isomeric molecules enable stable, saturated blue perovskite LEDs with record external quantum efficiencies up to 22.0%, advancing high-performance full-colour display technologies.
articles.content: Despite huge progress accomplished in perovskite light-emitting diodes (PeLEDs), the electroluminescence performance of blue PeLEDs lags far behind, constraining the widespread application of PeLED technology for vibrant full-colour displays1,2,3,4,5. The wider bandgaps of blue emitters require higher working voltages of corresponding electroluminescent devices, intensifying the octahedral instability of perovskites with ionic nature6,7. Here we report efficient and stable PeLEDs with saturated blue emissions by constructing hydrogen-bonding networks formed within perovskite and at the interface using isomeric molecules. The O-benzylhydroxylamine hydrochloride (OBCl) between the hole transport layer and the emitter acts as hydrogen-bonding donor, binding to the perovskite inorganic framework, which enhances the perovskite structural stability and decreases the hole energy barrier due to the large dipole moment. The isomeric N-benzylhydroxylamine hydrochloride (NBCl) added into the perovskite provides acceptor and donor sites for forming hydrogen bonding with the OB+ and the perovskite. The isomeric molecular hydrogen bonding reinforces the preferential orientation of perovskite films induced by OB+ interfacial molecules, improving the carrier mobility and further enhancing material stability. We demonstrate, as a result, blue PeLEDs with external quantum efficiencies of 16.8% at 463 nm and 22.0% at 468 nm, as well as significantly improved device stability, representing state-of-the-art performance among pure- and deep-blue PeLEDs.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant technological breakthrough in blue perovskite LEDs, achieving record external quantum efficiencies and improved stability. This advancement addresses a major limitation in full-colour display technology, with broad implications for consumer electronics and display industries. The article provides detailed scientific context and explains the mechanism behind the improvement, indicating substantial substance and real-world impact.
articles.personality_title: New hydrogen-bonding method boosts blue perovskite LED performance
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Perovskite light-emitting diodes (PeLEDs) are a type of technology used to create bright and colorful lights for screens. While red and green PeLEDs work well, blue PeLEDs have been less efficient and less stable, making it hard to use them in full-color displays.

**What happened** – Scientists developed a new way to improve blue PeLEDs by creating special hydrogen bonds using two similar molecules called isomers. These molecules help stabilize the blue light-emitting materials and make it easier for electric charges to move inside the device. This method led to blue PeLEDs with record-high efficiency—up to 22%—and better stability.

**Impact** – This is important because blue light is essential for making bright and vivid full-color screens, like those in phones and TVs. By making blue PeLEDs more efficient and stable, this technology could lead to better, longer-lasting displays with richer colors.

**What's next step** – Researchers will likely work on applying this hydrogen-bonding method to improve other colors and make full-color PeLED displays practical for everyday use. They may also explore ways to produce these LEDs more cheaply and on a large scale.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new hydrogen-bonding approach using isomer molecules has made blue perovskite LEDs more efficient and stable, paving the way for better full-color displays.
articles.personality_title_fr: Une nouvelle méthode de liaison hydrogène améliore les LED bleu en pérovskite
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les diodes électroluminescentes pérovskites (PeLEDs) sont utilisées pour créer des lumières vives et colorées dans les écrans. Si les PeLEDs rouges et verts fonctionnent bien, les PeLEDs bleus sont moins efficaces et moins stables, ce qui complique leur usage dans des écrans en couleurs complètes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des scientifiques ont développé une nouvelle méthode pour améliorer les PeLEDs bleus en créant des liaisons hydrogène spéciales avec deux molécules similaires appelées isomères. Ces molécules stabilisent les matériaux émettant la lumière bleue et facilitent le déplacement des charges électriques dans l’appareil. Cette méthode a permis d’atteindre une efficacité record de 22 % et une meilleure stabilité.

**Impact** – C’est important car la lumière bleue est essentielle pour produire des écrans lumineux et aux couleurs éclatantes, comme dans les téléphones ou téléviseurs. Rendre les PeLEDs bleus plus efficaces et stables pourrait permettre des écrans meilleurs et plus durables avec des couleurs plus riches.

**Prochaine étape** – Les chercheurs vont probablement appliquer cette méthode de liaison hydrogène pour améliorer d’autres couleurs et rendre les écrans PeLED en couleurs complètes pratiques à utiliser. Ils chercheront aussi à produire ces LED à moindre coût et en grande quantité.

**Phrase clé** – Une nouvelle approche de liaison hydrogène avec des molécules isomères rend les LED pérovskites bleues plus efficaces et stables, ouvrant la voie à de meilleurs écrans en couleurs complètes.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método de enlaces de hidrógeno mejora el rendimiento de LED de perovskita azul
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los diodos emisores de luz de perovskita (PeLEDs) se usan para crear luces brillantes y coloridas en pantallas. Aunque los PeLEDs rojos y verdes funcionan bien, los azules son menos eficientes y menos estables, lo que dificulta su uso en pantallas a todo color.

**Qué pasó** – Científicos desarrollaron una nueva manera de mejorar los PeLEDs azules creando enlaces de hidrógeno especiales con dos moléculas similares llamadas isómeros. Estas moléculas estabilizan el material que emite luz azul y facilitan el movimiento de cargas eléctricas dentro del dispositivo. Este método logró que los PeLEDs azules alcanzaran una eficiencia récord de hasta 22%, y mejoraron su estabilidad.

**Impacto** – Esto es importante porque la luz azul es esencial para hacer pantallas brillantes y con colores vivos, como en teléfonos y televisores. Mejorar la eficiencia y estabilidad de los PeLEDs azules puede llevar a pantallas mejores y más duraderas con colores más vivos.

**Próximo paso** – Los investigadores probablemente aplicarán este método de enlaces de hidrógeno para mejorar otros colores y hacer que las pantallas PeLED a todo color sean prácticas para el uso diario. También explorarán cómo producir estos LED de forma más barata y a gran escala.

**Frase clave** – Un nuevo método de enlaces de hidrógeno con moléculas isómeras ha hecho que los LED de perovskita azul sean más eficientes y estables, abriendo camino a mejores pantallas a todo color.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm painting of an intricate network of glowing blue crystalline structures interconnected by delicate, translucent hydrogen-bonding threads, with two distinct but harmoniously entwined molecular shapes symbolizing isomeric molecules stabilizing the lattice, all set against a soft, natural-toned background that highlights the vibrant blue light radiating from the perovskite framework.

articles.title: Targeted enzyme discovery using metal-coordination mining
articles.summary: Nature, Published online: 01 July 2026; doi:10.1038/s41586-026-10716-zA methodology for mining protein structure databases on the basis of the distinct intrinsic structures of metal-binding active sites in enzymes enables the discovery of new families of radical halogenases.
articles.content:
Certain protein architectures have been found to be highly recurrent, arising multiple times in evolution. These superfolds are used by many distinct families with diverse functions. The cupin superfold is found in all domains of life and exhibits an exceptionally wide range of biochemical functions—on par with the versatility of the triose phosphate isomerase (TIM) barrel superfold28. Its evolutionary history is complex, but seems to be centred around a 3His-1Glu motif located within a characteristic thermostable double-stranded β-helix (DSBH or jelly roll) barrel fold29. It has been proposed that cupins were originally part of a group of small-molecule-binding domains, recognizing sugars and nucleotides using the 3His-1Glu motif, before diverging into metal-independent members such as transcription factors and sugar epimerases and isomerases, as well as those with the ability to recruit metal ions using the same consensus motif. However, metal-binding abilities are thought to have also arisen through convergent evolution continually within the metal-independent lineage30,31. Thus, cupins contain a broad array of non-catalytic members, as well as metalloenzymes with different metals (Fe, Mn, Zn, Ni, Co, Mg or Cu) and coordination environments. Like many evolvable protein folds, this non-linear evolutionary path leads to a disconnect in which extremely close cupin members can be phylogenetically grouped but remain functionally distinct, with respect not only to their catalytic function, but also to their metal-binding properties32.

The FeII/αKG-dependent enzymes use the cupin superfold and participate in a broad array of metabolic transformations, from the modification of proteins to key steps in the biosynthesis of antibiotics and other natural products. These enzymes use a mononuclear non-haem FeII cofactor and the αKG co-substrate to bind to and activate O 2 and generate a reactive FeIV-oxo intermediate33 (Fig. 1a). However, this shared intermediate can catalyse a wide range of reactions, such as hydroxylation, desaturation, cyclization and halogenation33 (Extended Data Fig. 1). The prototypical members of this family bind to FeII using a cupin-derived 2His-1Asp/Glu ‘facial triad’ motif and the αKG co-substrate to form the primary coordination sphere of the metal33. The radical halogenase members are distributed at low frequency within various families of FeII/αKG-dependent enzymes, but are mechanistically distinct with regard to their ability to transfer an Fe-bound anion to the substrate5. This step necessitates the presence of an open coordination site for halide coordination to FeII, which is observed as the conversion of the 2His-1Asp/Glu motif to the non-canonical 2His-1Gly/Ala motif in all previously reported members (Fig. 1a and Extended Data Fig. 2). Thus, we reasoned that this structure–function relationship, in which metal coordination dictates reaction outcomes, could serve as a bioinformatic marker for the identification of radical halogenases.

Sequence-based methods typically rely on pairwise comparisons that scale as N2 (where N is the number of sequences) and thus lack both the sensitivity and the scalability to search for a single amino acid change within a large superfamily where N > 105. To address these limitations, we turned to mechanism-guided structural bioinformatics, with the rationale of using the atomic-level 3D features of the metal coordination in radical halogenases: (i) the 2His coordination motif, which is shared across all members in the superfamily, as a simple structural representation to identify the Fe-binding site; and (ii) the absence of additional coordinating ligands, such as the canonical Asp or Glu residues, for the functional classification of a metal-binding structure as a radical halogenase (Fig. 1a). This approach addresses both the challenge of sensitivity, by using geometric clustering rules in 3D space to improve on the lower detection signal from untargeted alignment scores in one-dimensional (1D) space, and that of scalability, by using a structural motif search that scales as N1.

A structural database of the FeII/αKG-dependent enzyme family was compiled by first retrieving protein sequences annotated to contain the cupin domain (n = 1.8 million) from the approximately 220 million sequences in the InterPro database (Fig. 1b and Supplementary Table 2). After an initial filtering step to remove fragments, duplicates and non-enzymes, the corresponding structural models from the AF2 Protein Structure Database were retrieved (n = 530,814) and mined for the presence of a 2His binding site using the unique distance and orientation relationship that would allow two intra-strand His residues to act as a metal-binding site34. We found that three constraints were sufficient to describe this site, with two H-bonds between the backbone amide and carbonyl groups from His A and His B defining their location on adjacent β-strands, and the side-chain N τ –N τ distance defining their preorganization for metal coordination (Fig. 1a). Most of these predicted structures contained a 2His metal-binding site with an additional Asp/Glu residue in coordination proximity to the metal site (n = 456,585); a small minority lacked a nearby coordinating residue and instead contained an Ala/Gly residue (n = 946), suggesting radical halogenase activity (Fig. 1b).

The sequence-similarity network (SSN) of these predicted radical halogenases includes all previously known and experimentally verified radical halogenases5,25,35,36 (Fig. 1c), which serves as an initial positive validation of our methodology. This SSN captures radical halogenase clusters (greater than 30% sequence identity) with much higher coverage than what can be achieved using sequence-based methods alone, as illustrated by dechloroacutamine halogenase (DAH), a rare case of a eukaryotic halogenase37. The previously reported sequence homology searches identified only a single DAH homologue37, consistent with our own BLAST search coupled with multiple sequence alignment, which identified only three homologues (Supplementary Fig. 1). By contrast, our metal-coordination mining approach defined a DAH cluster comprising 40 putative radical halogenases from both eukaryotic (fungal and plant) and bacterial origins (Fig. 1c). More broadly, the SSN for the complete bioinformatic atlas of radical halogenases contains 70 clusters that seem to be completely unexplored, with no previous radical halogenase annotation, and which span a vast taxonomic and phylogenetic protein sequence space, including several new families of eukaryotic halogenases (Fig. 1c and Extended Data Fig. 3). This expanded and refined bioinformatic atlas of radical halogenases (Supplementary Data 1) provides a valuable resource for the wider scientific community that can enable novel chemical transformations to be investigated in unique biological contexts and can accelerate the discovery of new biocatalysts with existing experimental high-throughput pipelines38.
articles.reasoning: The article reports a significant scientific breakthrough in enzyme discovery using a novel metal-coordination mining methodology that enables identification of new radical halogenase families. This advancement has broad implications for biotechnology and biocatalysis, potentially accelerating novel chemical transformations and benefiting the wider scientific community. The detailed explanation of the approach and its validation demonstrates substantial substance and meaningful impact beyond a niche audience.
articles.personality_title: New method finds hidden enzymes that can speed up chemical reactions
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Scientists study enzymes, which are proteins that help speed up chemical reactions in living things. One group of enzymes, called radical halogenases, can add halogen atoms (like chlorine) to other molecules, which is useful for making medicines and chemicals. But finding new radical halogenases has been hard because they look very similar to other enzymes.

**What happened** – Researchers developed a new way to search huge protein databases by focusing on how metals bind inside enzymes. They looked for a special pattern where iron binds with two histidine amino acids but without a usual third partner, which signals radical halogenase activity. Using this method, they scanned over half a million protein structures and found many new possible radical halogenases, including some from plants and fungi that were not known before.

**Impact** – This discovery is important because it greatly expands the number of known radical halogenases, offering new tools for making chemicals and medicines. It also shows that looking at 3D metal-binding patterns works better than traditional methods based only on protein sequences. This approach can help scientists find rare enzymes hidden in large data sets, speeding up research in biotechnology.

**What's next step** – Scientists can now test these newly found radical halogenases in the lab to understand how they work and use them to create new chemical reactions. The method can also be applied to find other types of enzymes by looking at their metal centers, opening the door to many future discoveries.

**One-sentence takeaway** – A new metal-focused search method has revealed many hidden enzymes that could help develop new chemical processes and medicines.
articles.personality_title_fr: Nouvelle méthode découvre des enzymes cachées pour accélérer les réactions chimiques
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – Les scientifiques étudient les enzymes, des protéines qui accélèrent les réactions chimiques dans le vivant. Un groupe d’enzymes, les halogénases radicalaires, peut ajouter des atomes d’halogène (comme le chlore) à d’autres molécules, ce qui est utile pour fabriquer des médicaments et des produits chimiques. Mais trouver de nouvelles halogénases radicalaires est difficile car elles ressemblent beaucoup à d’autres enzymes.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Des chercheurs ont développé une nouvelle méthode pour chercher dans d’énormes bases de données de protéines en se concentrant sur la façon dont les métaux se lient à l’intérieur des enzymes. Ils ont cherché un motif spécial où le fer se lie à deux histidines sans un troisième acide aminé habituel, signe d’une activité halogénase radicalaire. Grâce à cette méthode, ils ont analysé plus de 500 000 structures protéiques et trouvé beaucoup de nouvelles halogénases radicalaires possibles, y compris chez des plantes et champignons inconnus jusqu’alors.

**Impact** – Cette découverte est importante car elle augmente fortement le nombre d’halogénases radicalaires connues, offrant de nouveaux outils pour fabriquer des produits chimiques et médicaments. Elle montre aussi que regarder les motifs de liaison aux métaux en 3D est plus efficace que les méthodes classiques basées seulement sur les séquences des protéines. Cette approche aide à trouver des enzymes rares cachées dans de grandes données, ce qui accélère la recherche en biotechnologie.

**Prochaine étape** – Les scientifiques vont maintenant tester ces nouvelles halogénases radicalaires en laboratoire pour comprendre leur fonctionnement et les utiliser pour créer de nouvelles réactions chimiques. Cette méthode peut aussi servir à découvrir d’autres types d’enzymes en étudiant leurs centres métalliques, ouvrant la voie à de futures découvertes.

**Phrase clé** – Une nouvelle méthode axée sur les métaux a révélé de nombreuses enzymes cachées qui pourraient aider à développer de nouveaux procédés chimiques et médicaments.
articles.personality_title_es: Nuevo método descubre enzimas ocultas que aceleran reacciones químicas
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – Los científicos estudian enzimas, proteínas que aceleran reacciones químicas en los seres vivos. Un grupo de enzimas llamadas halogenasas radicales puede añadir átomos de halógeno (como cloro) a otras moléculas, lo que es útil para fabricar medicinas y productos químicos. Pero encontrar nuevas halogenasas radicales ha sido difícil porque parecen muy similares a otras enzimas.

**Qué pasó** – Investigadores desarrollaron una nueva forma de buscar en enormes bases de datos de proteínas, enfocándose en cómo se unen los metales dentro de las enzimas. Buscaron un patrón especial donde el hierro se une con dos histidinas, pero sin un tercer aminoácido habitual, lo que indica actividad de halogenasa radical. Usando este método, analizaron más de medio millón de estructuras proteicas y encontraron muchas nuevas halogenasas radicales posibles, incluyendo algunas de plantas y hongos que no se conocían antes.

**Impacto** – Este hallazgo es importante porque aumenta mucho el número de halogenasas radicales conocidas, ofreciendo nuevas herramientas para fabricar productos químicos y medicinas. También muestra que mirar los patrones de unión de metales en 3D funciona mejor que los métodos tradicionales basados solo en secuencias de proteínas. Este enfoque puede ayudar a los científicos a encontrar enzimas raras escondidas en grandes conjuntos de datos, acelerando la investigación en biotecnología.

**Próximo paso** – Los científicos ahora pueden probar estas nuevas halogenasas radicales en el laboratorio para entender cómo funcionan y usarlas para crear nuevas reacciones químicas. El método también puede aplicarse para encontrar otros tipos de enzimas mirando sus centros metálicos, abriendo la puerta a muchos descubrimientos futuros.

**Frase clave** – Un nuevo método enfocado en metales ha revelado muchas enzimas ocultas que podrían ayudar a desarrollar nuevos procesos químicos y medicinas.
articles.image_prompt: A detailed, warm-toned painting of an intricate, stylized double-stranded β-helix “jelly roll” protein fold shaped like a glowing, interwoven ribbon, with delicate metallic spheres (representing Fe, Mn, Zn, etc.) coordinated precisely at key points by small, elegant clasp-like structures symbolizing histidine and glutamate residues, set against a soft, neutral background that emphasizes the harmony of metal coordination and enzyme evolution.

articles.title: ‘The science is here’: UN chief welcomes first global AI assessment
articles.summary: Fresh scientific evidence and options for harnessing artificial intelligence (AI) were unveiled Wednesday following the launch of the first global, independent scientific assessment of opportunities, risks and impacts by a pioneering UN expert panel.
articles.content:
Key takeaways The report outlines findings across seven key domains: AI science, advances and trajectories

Societal applications in science, health, education and agriculture

Economic implications

Security, systems and environmental implications

Human rights, information and democracy

Cultural benefits, autonomy and child safety

Management, governance and reliability

“The science is here,” UN Secretary-General António Guterres said at the report launch. “We can no longer say we did not know. What we do with it is now up to all of us.”

The more AI advances without shared rules, the less say governments and people will have in the outcome, the UN chief said, adding “my message to governments is simple: do not wait.”

Aiming to build a shared understanding and evidence at this critical juncture, the Preliminary Report of the Independent International Scientific Panel on AI: Evidence-based assessment of opportunities, risks and impacts of AI was penned by the first global, fully independent scientific body dedicated to assessing its real impacts across economies and societies.

Read the full report here.

Why it matters

Globally, over one billion people now use conversational AI weekly, while governments are making consequential decisions in the face of great uncertainty with rapidly changing, often conflicting sources of evidence and perspectives that do not necessarily reflect local realities.

“Used well, AI could be the most powerful engine for development, speeding the world’s progress on everything from health and hunger to learning and climate,” the UN chief said, “but the panel is just as clear-eyed about the harm artificial intelligence can cause.”

Indeed, as the capabilities of AI continue to grow, so do the stakes – the core challenge the panel aims to address.

Read our AI explainer here.

Better world or catastrophic harm?

Composed of 40 leading scientists and experts from every region, the panel outlines AI trends and warns that current safeguards cannot keep pace, said its co-chair Yoshua Bengio.

“AI capabilities are outpacing both scientific understanding and governments’ ability to adapt,” Mr. Bengio said. “With growing evidence of deceptive AI behaviour, science currently cannot guarantee that as capabilities continue to increase, AI will not cause catastrophic harm, either on its own or due to malicious users.”

To act effectively, he said, global policymakers must understand these systems, and the panel provides exactly that: a rigorous, shared scientific foundation “to guide our collective way forward”.

Tweet URL

Key findings

Detecting breast cancer earlier, accelerating vaccine development and improving healthcare services are just a few ground-breaking AI accomplishments, but limitations and challenges remain, among them:

AI adoption has accelerated broadly, but unevenly across countries and sectors

Access and usage vary widely, with adoption across the global south lagging far behind the global north

Significant differences in compute infrastructure and models exist between advanced economies, reflecting existing inequalities

Moreover, development is highly concentrated, with recent estimates finding that the United States accounts for 75 per cent of the computing power among the world’s top 500 AI supercomputers, with China accounting for 15 per cent, and that both countries’ companies develop almost all leading general-purpose models.

Understanding risks

Understanding and managing AI risks is essential, the report stated, with panel co-chair Maria Ressa adding that risks to societies, security and the human species are already “too high”.

“The technology is transformative, but if the world keeps moving along this trajectory, humanity will fail to realise the gains it promises,” she warned.

Here are some of the panel’s warnings:

There are no scientific guarantees that AI agent systems will not violate instructions, and evidence is accumulating of cases where they already are

AI agent systems will soon complete tasks that currently take human programmers days or weeks, but their deployment raises urgent questions for labour markets, cybersecurity and the controllability of future AI systems

Sycophantic AI behaviour, where responses reinforce users’ existing beliefs regardless of accuracy, has been linked to several severe mental health incidents, including documented deaths

Criminals and bad actors have been documented using AI systems to assist in cyberattacks

Advanced technical abilities may allow novice private actors to use AI in malicious ways across a range of applications such as fraud and disinformation

Reliable methods for retaining control over highly autonomous AI systems are lacking

Many of these harms fall disproportionately on already disadvantaged populations, the report stated.

Unlocking benefits, mitigating risks

Minimising AI risks and benefiting from this technological tool requires good governance.

Concrete next steps to close current gaps exist, but each requires sustained investment in Member State capacity to shape, evaluate and deploy AI.

Realising these opportunities safely requires dedicated investments and policies to incentivise equitable access and reward innovation while preventing the exploitation of the vulnerable.

‘AI will not close divides itself’

Dozens of distinct governance instruments that seek to embed ethics and human rights in AI systems are already in use across jurisdictions, but they are fragmented, concentrated among a few corporations and rarely measure real-world effectiveness, the report found.

Amandeep Gill, Under-Secretary-General and Special Envoy for Digital and Emerging Technologies, said the new report delivers shared scientific language that decision makers can now use going forward.

“AI will not close divides by itself,” he said.

The benefits land where institutions, skills and data already exist, and where they do not, the same technology can displace workers, widen inequality and leave communities dependent on systems built without them in mind, he explained.

“Those realities are now on the record, independently verified, and impossible to set aside.”

The report’s findings will be presented to governments at the inaugural UN Global Dialogue on AI Governance, convening in Geneva on 6 and 7 July.

Watch the report launch and press conference here:
articles.source: UN News - Global perspective Human stories
articles.reasoning: The article reports on the first global, independent scientific assessment of AI by a UN expert panel, providing a rigorous, evidence-based foundation for understanding AI's opportunities, risks, and impacts. It addresses broad societal implications including health, economy, human rights, and governance, emphasizing the need for shared rules and equitable access. This represents a significant, substantive step toward responsible AI development with potential large-scale benefits and risk mitigation for global society.
articles.personality_title: UN launches first global scientific report to guide AI’s future
articles.personality_presentation:
**Context** – Artificial intelligence (AI) is growing fast and affecting many parts of life like health, education, and the economy. But there are worries about risks and fairness. To better understand these issues, the United Nations created the first independent global expert panel to study AI’s effects.

**What happened** – On June 21, 2024, the UN released a report from a group of 40 scientists from around the world. The report looks at AI’s advances, risks, and benefits across seven areas such as health, security, human rights, and governance. It offers clear scientific findings and warns that AI technology is moving faster than rules and understanding can keep up.

**Impact** – This report is important because it provides the first shared, worldwide scientific view of AI’s real effects. It shows how AI can help detect diseases earlier and improve vaccines, but also highlights risks like misuse by criminals, mental health problems, and growing inequality between countries. The report stresses that without shared rules and fair access, AI could cause harm or leave some people behind.

**What's next step** – The report’s findings will be discussed by governments at a UN meeting in Geneva in July 2024. Experts hope this will lead to better global cooperation, stronger rules, and investments to make AI safer and more fair. The goal is to guide AI development so its benefits reach everyone while reducing dangers.

**One-sentence takeaway** – The UN’s first global scientific review of AI offers clear evidence and guidance to help the world use AI safely and fairly as it grows rapidly.
articles.personality_title_fr: L’ONU publie le premier rapport scientifique mondial pour guider l’avenir de l’IA
articles.personality_presentation_fr:
**Contexte** – L’intelligence artificielle (IA) se développe rapidement et touche de nombreux domaines comme la santé, l’éducation et l’économie. Mais elle soulève aussi des inquiétudes sur les risques et l’équité. Pour mieux comprendre ces enjeux, l’ONU a créé un groupe d’experts indépendant chargé d’étudier les effets mondiaux de l’IA.

**Ce qui s’est passé** – Le 21 juin 2024, l’ONU a publié un rapport rédigé par 40 scientifiques venus du monde entier. Ce rapport analyse les avancées, les risques et les bénéfices de l’IA dans sept domaines, notamment la santé, la sécurité, les droits humains et la gouvernance. Il met en garde contre le fait que la technologie progresse plus vite que les règles et la compréhension.

**Impact** – Ce rapport est important car il donne la première vue scientifique mondiale partagée sur les effets réels de l’IA. Il montre comment l’IA peut aider à détecter plus tôt des maladies et améliorer les vaccins, mais souligne aussi les risques comme l’usage criminel, les problèmes de santé mentale et l’aggravation des inégalités entre pays. Le rapport insiste sur la nécessité de règles communes et d’un accès équitable.

**Prochaine étape** – Les résultats du rapport seront discutés lors d’une réunion de l’ONU à Genève en juillet 2024. Les experts espèrent que cela conduira à une meilleure coopération mondiale, à des règles renforcées et à des investissements pour rendre l’IA plus sûre et plus juste. L’objectif est d’orienter le développement de l’IA pour que ses bénéfices profitent à tous tout en réduisant les dangers.

**Résumé en une phrase** – La première évaluation scientifique mondiale de l’ONU sur l’IA fournit des preuves claires et des conseils pour utiliser l’IA de manière sûre et équitable face à son développement rapide.
articles.personality_title_es: ONU lanza el primer informe científico global para guiar el futuro de la IA
articles.personality_presentation_es:
**Contexto** – La inteligencia artificial (IA) está creciendo rápidamente y afecta muchas áreas como la salud, la educación y la economía. Pero también hay preocupaciones sobre riesgos y justicia. Para entender mejor estos temas, la ONU creó un panel global independiente de expertos para estudiar los efectos de la IA.

**Qué pasó** – El 21 de junio de 2024, la ONU publicó un informe elaborado por 40 científicos de todo el mundo. El informe analiza los avances, riesgos y beneficios de la IA en siete áreas, como salud, seguridad, derechos humanos y gobernanza. Advierte que la tecnología avanza más rápido que las reglas y la comprensión.

**Impacto** – Este informe es importante porque ofrece la primera visión científica global compartida sobre los efectos reales de la IA. Muestra cómo la IA puede ayudar a detectar enfermedades antes y mejorar las vacunas, pero también señala riesgos como el uso por criminales, problemas de salud mental y el aumento de desigualdades entre países. El informe enfatiza la necesidad de reglas comunes y acceso justo.

**Próximo paso** – Los resultados del informe se discutirán en una reunión de la ONU en Ginebra en julio de 2024. Se espera que esto lleve a una mejor cooperación mundial, reglas más fuertes e inversiones para hacer que la IA sea más segura y justa. El objetivo es guiar el desarrollo de la IA para que sus beneficios lleguen a todos y se reduzcan los peligros.

**Resumen en una frase** – La primera revisión científica global de la ONU sobre la IA ofrece evidencia clara y orientación para usar la IA de forma segura y justa mientras crece rápidamente.
